articles.full_text:
Hyderabad: Welcome showers bring relief from killer heatwave; IMD issues orange alert
Malkajgiri’s Vinayak Nagar Ward Office recorded the highest rainfall at 21.3 mm, followed closely by the BHEL Factory area in Sangareddy at 17.0 mm.
By Newsmeter NetworkRepresentative Image
Hyderabad: A welcome rainfall brought much-needed relief to people from the sweltering heat wave in Hyderabad.
Malkajgiri’s Vinayak Nagar Ward Office recorded the highest rainfall at 21.3 mm, followed closely by the BHEL Factory area in Sangareddy at 17.0 mm.
Other areas that witnessed significant rainfall included Shapur Nagar and Jeedimetla in Quthbullapur, which registered 16.0 mm and 15.0 mm, respectively, while Rajiv Gruhakalpa in Kukatpally saw 13.8 mm. The Serilingampally circle was also well covered, with Miyapur recording 13.0 mm and Lingampally recording 12.3 mm. Kukatpally recorded 10.5 mm, and Alwal’s PHC Center registered 9.8 mm, while Banjara Hills and Jubilee Hills both saw 7.3 mm of rainfall.
While Hyderabad enjoyed a pleasant break, the weather was far more intense across the wider Telangana state, where heavy downpours lashed several southern districts during the night of May 26th.
Meanwhile, due to adverse weather conditions, the electrical lines in several areas have been damaged. The TSPCDL teams are also on the field to restore the electric supply.
⚠️ Due to adverse weather conditions, electrical infrastructure has been damaged, leading to power supply interruptions at various locations in Vikarabd town area of Vikarabad Circle.@tgspdcl teams are on the field and working continuously to restore supply at the earliest. All… pic.twitter.com/Xne5cvN9lM
— TGSPDCL (@tgspdcl) May 27, 2026
Districts affected
The district-level data revealed that Bhoothpur in Mahabubnagar was the hardest hit, recording a staggering 112.0 mm of rainfall, followed by Koilkonda in the same district at 107.8 mm. Amarachintha in Wanaparthy at 88.5 mm, while Nagarkurnool’s Thimmajipeta saw 88.3 mm. Other significant figures included 72.8 mm at Koheda in Siddipet, 64.0 mm at Kaloor Timmanadoddi in Jogulamba Gadwal, and 62.5 mm at Gundlapalle in Nalgonda. Narayanpet’s Utkoor and Vikarabad’s Doulthabad also received considerable rainfall, measuring 59.8 mm and 53.8 mm, respectively.
The relief, however, comes with a note of caution as the Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued an `orange warning’ for the morning of May 27.
According to the nowcast issued at 7:00 AM, the atmospheric instability is expected to persist through the early hours. Residents in Jogulamba Gadwal, Mahabubnagar, Narayanpet, Rangareddy, Sangareddy, Vikarabad, and Wanaparthy have been warned of moderate thunderstorms accompanied by gusty winds reaching speeds of 41 to 61 kmph. Meanwhile, Hyderabad and surrounding districts like Jangaon, Karimnagar, Siddipet, and Warangal are very likely to witness continued light to moderate rain and thunderstorms with wind speeds up to 40 kmph.
Meanwhile, the weather alerts from the IMD and the independent weather experts have also come in late, the thunderstorm warnings have come only towards late night.
Advisory
The IMD advised residents to remain vigilant. Farmers are urged to move harvested crops to safe locations, and commuters are warned to expect
traffic delays of 10 to 15 minutes due to slippery roads and low visibility. The public is strictly advised against seeking shelter under trees or near weak structures during thunderstorms.
